item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from here and is licensed under All Rights Reserved. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so for me, I see two really good places for potential crosslinks here. The first would be a link from the first mention of the Ethics Committee to the EC Orientation tale. Why? Cause I feel that tale in question sets the tone for the version of the EC portrayed in this skip. The second link would be from the first mention of ALEXYLVA to the Parable of the Wayward Prince, since that tale series is an effective way of fostering a wikiwalk for those interested in delving down the AU rabbit hole. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

SCP-1998 is to be contained in a High Value Storage Locker in Site 38. Testing of SCP-1998's effects are to be carried out only according to the following protocols. First, a request is to be filed with the Foundation Ethics Committee, which will provide both the appropriate D-class personnel and the parameters under which experimentation can be carried out. Second, all aspects of every experiment are to be monitored in person by one representative of the Ethics Committee and broadcast live via closed-circuit camera to the remainder of the Committee. Third, under no circumstances is SCP-1998 to be used for anything other than experimental testing; any attempt to use SCP-1998 to alter the free will of D-class personnel for the benefit of either individual members or departments of the Foundation will be considered grounds for immediate demotion and reassignment.

To this:

SCP-1998 is to be contained in a High Value Storage Locker in Site 38. Testing of SCP-1998's effects are to be carried out only according to the following protocols. First, a request is to be filed with the Foundation Ethics Committee, which will provide both the appropriate D-class personnel and the parameters under which experimentation can be carried out. Second, all aspects of every experiment are to be monitored in person by one representative of the Ethics Committee and broadcast live via closed-circuit camera to the remainder of the Committee. Third, under no circumstances is SCP-1998 to be used for anything other than experimental testing; any attempt to use SCP-1998 to alter the free will of D-class personnel for the benefit of either individual members or departments of the Foundation will be considered grounds for immediate demotion and reassignment.

Changing this:

DESTINATION:
ALEXYLVA UNIVERSITY
CENTRAL ACADEMIC AUTHORITY
EQUIPMENT REQUISITIONS DEPARTMENT
LIII FIRST CIRCLE
ALEXANDRIA OF FORESTS

To this:

DESTINATION:
ALEXYLVA UNIVERSITY
CENTRAL ACADEMIC AUTHORITY
EQUIPMENT REQUISITIONS DEPARTMENT
LIII FIRST CIRCLE
ALEXANDRIA OF FORESTS

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A unique take on the cookie-cutter raygun idea. Its existence is troubling enough that the Ethics Committee (who presumably has approved any number of horrible experiments for the greater good) wants to keep a close eye on it to prevent abuse. Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would be soooooo useful in real life……..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its an updated version of the control collars?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I very much enjoy this, glad to see it featured.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "A request was put in to use the device on 682 to command him to destroy himself. It was denied as, honestly, we can't deal with losing more staff this month when this inevitably fails"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The average D class life is 30-31 days so… I guess it could be said for the rest of there lives

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice spin on the ethics Commitee! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the explanation!

+ Wall-o-text
- Hide

I admit that what you have there is a very solid idea with very interesting implications. I actually enjoyed reading this post more than the skip. Because unfortunately, the skip did not manage to convey the idea of a mind-control-commonplace society to me. Maybe I'm just not as familiar with Alexylva as I should be, or maybe I'm dense, but this came across as a "Wondertainment" sort of skip… like some misguided company out there thinks there's actually a market for these things. And with that interpretation in mind, it falls flat, and the horrible implications are lost.

But now that I know what I know, I'll go read a few other Alexylva skips…

961. Upvote. 1080. Strong upvote. 877 Upvote.

So I do see where this is coming from now. Maybe if I had read this as my fourth or fifth Alexylva skip I might've instinctively upvoted. But I didn't, and I stand by the fact that skips should stand well-enough on their own, and I don't think this one quite makes the grade. The others all have some other interesting, clever, additional hook where I feel you could go in blind, knowing nothing about Alexyvla, and still find them enjoyable. This one I feel requires that prerequisite knowledge.

Also I pity you for having worked at a daycare.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconded.

And +1 for "Is that a fucking raygun?" "Yes. That is a raygun." "…Fucking ALEXYLVA…" Talk about a tagline that'll hook you in….I was sold as soon as I read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, not a very innovative idea, but well-written. Also, +1 for the "Ethics Comitee" and the Alexylva University :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, you're under no obligation to apologize for or temper a downvote in any way. You're no more "pissing in the wind" than the average voter in an American election, and there's a helluva smaller voterbase here. And you're allowed to express your opinion however you see fit.

First of all, I don't see "trite" in the sense of "done before" as a negative. "Thing what lets you control minds" has been done before in sci-fi, and will continue to be done as long as the idea of not having control over our minds is, y'know, creepy as hell. Which it will continue to be. My particular angle on all of the University objects was designed to explore the idea of "what if there was a society whose position on civil liberties and personal freedom was completely opposite ours to the extent that a company could mass-produce mind-reprogramming tools that look like weapons…and then give them away like business cards to advertise how good they are at it." There's nothing resembling artifice here. It's not designed to reprogram your mind with any subtlety and convince you to be on anyone's side. It's designed to punish disagreement and disobedience, force you to behave how they want you to behave, and force you to watch yourself doing it. There's no amnestic quality here. It's the human equivalent of putting your dog's face in poop in the living room and it's designed for use on children; you're supposed to remember what's happening, so that you fall into line later without needing it. The society of Alexylva University is also designed to resemble Plato's Republic as much as possible, just to remind everyone that these aren't even new ideas.

As for your other specific complaints: There aren't any interesting test logs because the description describes what it does, and any test log would just reinforce that without adding anything (as I expect most Descriptions would for Safe-level items in my headcanon, since the item basically follows pretty straightforward rules and examples shouldn't be overly necessary). The Description…eh, it's three paragraphs long. I wrote it as concisely as I could. The recovery is pretty boilerplate Alexylva; most of their objects have similar recovery stories in keeping with how the University works as an origin of SCP objects, and the document needs to stand alone, so it's a variation on a theme. The University connection is designed to reinforce the whole "yeah, there's about a million more of these somewhere, and they're really being used in classrooms right now" thing, so…it is what it is. I didn't even think the containment procedures were that particularly outstanding in this case; they seemed mostly common-sense to me. The Ethics Committee is doing what I imagine the Ethics Committee does, which clearly isn't in line with how Voct and some others imagine it, but I respectfully disagree and I can live with that.

Okay, that's my belated wall of text on this one. I should point out that most of the Alexylva objects came from me thinking up shit I wish I had when I was working at a daycare for two years, so that's the perspective it's coming from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

I realize I'm in the minority and that this is pissing in the wind, but I really don't see what the big fuss is. Did I miss a link to another document somewhere in the description? (Because I've done that before.) Everyone here admits that the idea is trite. Most are saying that the execution is what makes this stand out. Okay… well what about it? Unless I'm missing something, there are no interesting test logs, the description is long-winded, boring, and predictable, the recovery is so uninteresting that it should have been left out, and the University connection doesn't really add anything. I admit that the containment procedures were done well, even exceptional, but that's not enough for me to upvote. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, man, I found this one a long time ago, and now it's featured? Bravo. (Feel like a hippie.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feels simutaniously terrifying, and incredibly useful for teaching. A bit too convienient, if you ask me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it does require approximately five minutes between uses for cooldown or recharging of some sort.

…Maybe change this to something along the lines of "five minutes between uses, possibly for cooldown or recharging?" It feels a little un-clinical as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish i could upvote this comment XD

item.title: Sorry, Adam
item.content:encoded: but that's not an order…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this highly interesting, and completely understand why the Ethics Committee is mentioned. I love the University mention, I almost immediately upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My interpretation is: That's standard procedure.

And, indeed, was probably the procedure used for SCP-1998 at first.

But 1998 was too tempting to the unethical researchers, including researchers who were working with other SCPs that required D-class (and some who were just doing practical jokes). So the Ethics Committee has taken over management of this particular SCP, in order to prevent further abuse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Ethics Committee wouldn't make recommendations before the experiments are done.

The Ethics Committee would watch what gets done, and make little notes. And sometimes they'd ask researchers or guards to explain themselves.

And the researchers would sigh, because the Ethics Committee is a huge waste of everyone's time, and explain what they'd just done and why. "This has obviously been designed to work on humans. We need to find out what it can and can't do" is a good explanation. "Getting those two D-class to make out with each other was fuckin' HILARIOUS!" is not.

And the Ethics Committee would nod, and say "thank you", and then the researcher would go back to work.

And the first researcher would continue working with the SCP.

And the second researcher would find himself reassigned to another project, with a lower salary.

The Foundation's Ethics Committee is reactive, not proactive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's part of why I liked this article. It's not because they care about the D-Class, because they still let the researchers use them for experiments, it's just that they don't do anything more than they need to. They justify doing the terrible things so that the rest of the world gets to sleep at night. This does not include allowing the researchers to have slaves, like you said. That doesn't help the Foundation learn anything or further their goals, but the temptation might be strong enough for some that they need the additional guidelines to keep people focused on what they should be doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, in this article, the role of the Ethics Committee isn't so much to make sure D-Class personnel are treated humanely (though it is intentionally phrased that way in places). It's more to make sure that random-ass researchers don't start brainwashing themselves a bunch of mindless slaves. Not even because they give a shit if the D-Class's are slaves; just because having slaves isn't one of the perks of being a researcher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that's why I like the Ethics Committee. It means that somewhere in the background, someone is actually doing the devil's math. When the Foundation commits an atrocity it is because someone higher up ran the numbers, considered the options and gave the go-ahead. That makes "by any means necessary" far more bone-chilling.

Most especially because no one actually seems to know the Committee's ethical standards, let alone get any chance to discuss them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like how it's used here, hence my downvote. However, Voct's tale is awesome and scary, because in his interpretation, it's less "ethics committee" and more "internal secret police" . Which is scarier than a lot of the objects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like the Ethics Committe, but I'm aparantly in the minority. The Foundation obviously subscribes to an "ends justify the means" mentality, hence 110-montauk etc ad nauseum. Once you've accepted that philosophy, then factor in that the "ends" of the Foundation are protecting all of humanity from unimaginable horror, suffering, and death…well, I just can't think of anything that an Ethics Committee could prohibit and make stick.

Ethics Committee: You can't do that! The test subject will lose most of his skin, have his nerve fibers woven into poison ivy macrame, and remain concious through the entire process. And why does his mother have to watch?
Random Scientist: Yes, that is unfortunate. But she is the most familiar with the test subject. Who better to notice any minor anomalies? Besides, this test may lead to finally decommisioning 682! I think that's worth the sacrifice of any one man, don't you?
Ethics Committee:…Very well. I'll notify janitorial to bring the big corpse-bucket.

Whatever, just my opinion, no canon, and all that.

I still really like the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd personally like this more without the mind control component, with it just functioning to get through people's thick skulls. As-is, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to upvote when I read the containment procedures, I never once thought I shouldn't upvote. This article from top to bottom is nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 indeed for Ethics Committee. I'd +1 again for proper use of the University, but alas, I have but one vote to give.

I feel…empty. Like I failed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was on the fence about this one, but I think what finally secured my upvote were the containment procedures involving the Ethics committee. Apart from liking seeing them used more, I like that they are clearly trying to use this device in a reasonably responsible manner. Not everyone in the Foundation is a complete monster, and they only do what they need to do, nothing more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted the moment I caught wind of University involvement. Great stuff!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reject points it at 076-2
<Reject> AAAAAAAAABLEEEE
<Reject> I FUCKED YOUR MOM

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was reading 'universiverse' as 'wait, it says universe but universe looks weird…'

God I think I need more sleep. Except I woke up at 2 PM.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Outstanding, a perfect addition to the universiverse

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is proof that execution is at least 75% of an article (the other 25% is having a good idea to begin with). Sure, a Thing What Let You Control People's Minds has been done by pretty much every science-fiction ever, but the way this has been pulled off makes it work. +1

Oh, and interesting mention of the Ethics Committee there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where it says "Will be remembered for the rest of the subject's life"—unless they've actually tested this for the duration of a subject's life, it should say indefinitely or something like that. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's like a mind affecting version of the Righteous Bison? Could've gone good or bad, but the execuiton is better than I expected. +1 for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first i though "oh its just a magic do what i say gun, how very original" but the article makes it work quite well, and mention of the university made in way more interesting as a product that was intentionally made, and probably mass produced.

+1
